C001.narrative: Jasmine's Route 47 rerouted through Mission Street, adding 35 minutes each way. Called her neighbor to cover after-school pickup.
Jasmine adjusts her schedule. Her neighbor Mrs. Ortega now collects the kids Tuesday and Thursday. Jasmine covers her neighbor's Saturday errands in return.
Atmospheric river adds another 20 minutes on flood-prone Mission Street reroute. Jasmine is running on four hours of sleep.
Block A reopens. Jasmine's route slightly improves, but Block B closure creates a new pinch point near Civic Center.
Jasmine files a formal SFMTA rider feedback report about the Route 47 detour impacts on school pickup windows.
Jasmine calculates she has driven 900 extra miles in 6 months. Her bus is showing signs of extra wear on the Mission detour route.
Jasmine speaks at the SFMTA midpoint community meeting. Gets 3 minutes on the mic. The room applauds when she says 'my kids pay the price for detours that were never planned for families.'
Block B clears. Jasmine's commute improves by 12 minutes. End is in sight.
Jasmine can see Block C from her bus window. Three more months. She starts counting down.
Utility pipe burst causes another week of detour. Jasmine texts her union rep about schedule impact pay.
Jasmine can see the new road surface on Blocks A and B from the driver's seat. Almost there. She lets herself feel hopeful.
Route 47 fully restored on Van Ness. Jasmine's commute is 8 minutes faster than pre-construction. She makes pickup at 3:15pm. First time in a year.

C002.narrative: Ricci's Italian Kitchen entrance partially blocked by construction hoarding. Lunch crowd down 30%. Tony puts a handmade sign on the sidewalk.
Tony applies for the City's small business disruption fund. $15,000 — barely covers two weeks of losses. He submits anyway.
Atmospheric river keeps customers away. Tony uses the slowdown to repaint the dining room ceiling. First time in eight years.
Block B construction moves directly in front of Ricci's. Entrance completely barricaded by staging equipment. Revenue down 60%. Tony is drawing on personal savings.
Tony's wife suggests closing for the summer and taking a trip. Tony refuses. His father opened this restaurant in 1978 and he is not giving up.
Tony withdraws $40,000 from his retirement account to cover payroll. His accountant says they have 4 months of runway.
Tony speaks at the DPW community midpoint review. Demands contractor be required to maintain customer access pathways. Gets a written commitment.
Block B moves on. Tony's front entrance is clear. He puts out a 'WE'RE BACK' chalkboard on the sidewalk. A few regulars trickle in.
Revenue back to 55% of normal. Tony brings back his part-time waiter. First rehire since construction started.
Tony hears about the pipe burst. He cannot take another delay. Calls his City Council rep directly.
Revenue at 80% of baseline and climbing. Tony orders new tablecloths. The regulars are coming back and bringing friends.
Ricci's Italian Kitchen has its best Saturday in 14 months. Tony's wife brings in a homemade tiramisu for the kitchen staff. He does not tell her his retirement account is still down $40,000.

C003.narrative: Sofia watches the Block A excavation from a distance, waiting to hear back on her contract inspector application.
Sofia gets the call. SF Public Works assigns her to the Block A inspection team. She starts Monday. First day she drives the route three times to memorize it.
Sofia documents the atmospheric river impact on freshly-excavated trenches. Files a delay risk report with DPW project manager. He takes it seriously.
Sofia's drainage inspection report leads to a quality checkpoint being added for Block B. Site foreman gives her a nod of respect.
Sofia raises the dust issue at the site safety meeting. HEPA dust suppression installed within 72 hours. Amy Park's complaint had reached the DPW hotline the same week.
Sofia is asked to formally document the contractor milestone miss. She does so meticulously. Her report becomes the basis for the red flag filing.
Sofia's delay documentation is cited in the official DPW midpoint report. Her supervisor recommends her for a permanent role.
Sofia oversees the Block B handover inspection. Finds 3 minor issues — all corrected before sign-off. Project manager buys the team burritos.
Sofia receives her permanent DPW offer letter — salary $94,000. She calls her mom in Fresno and cries a little. Cal Poly loans looking payable.
Sofia is called back from her new permanent role to inspect the pipe burst damage on Block C. Her expertise is now formally recognized across the project.
Sofia is now supervising two junior inspectors. Fastest advancement in the Van Ness project team.
Sofia walks the full 2.3km at the ribbon-cutting ceremony. She supervised every meter of it. Her mom drives up from Fresno for the opening.

C004.narrative: Earl's walking route to CPMC on Van Ness is now blocked by excavation barriers. Has to take a longer sidewalk route with his walker.
Earl's nephew offers to drive him to appointments. Earl accepts Tuesdays only. He is proud and stubborn the rest of the time.
Rain keeps Earl indoors. His neighbor from the SRO picks up his prescriptions. Earl makes her tea in return.
Earl marks 8 months remaining on his wall calendar. Each day is a small red X.
Earl's diabetes check shows slightly elevated readings. Doctor attributes it to reduced walking and stress.
Earl's neighbor Linda has started accompanying him to his CPMC appointments every two weeks. He buys her a muffin from the hospital cafe each time.
Earl watches the DPW midpoint meeting livestream on his phone. A supervisor mentions 'senior pedestrian access' and Earl feels seen for the first time.
Earl marks Month 8 on his calendar. 4 more months. He tells Linda he can see the end from here.
Earl's doctor clears him for short walks. He practices his route in the SRO corridor, preparing for when the sidewalk reopens.
Earl hears about the pipe burst on the radio. Says to Linda: 'That's San Francisco — there's always one more thing.' Not angry, just tired.
Earl can see the construction winding down from his window. He asks his nephew to be there for the opening. Nephew books the day off work.
Earl walks to CPMC on Van Ness for the first time in 12 months. The new sidewalk has a textured ADA strip and a covered bus shelter right on his block. He takes it slow. He smiles the whole way.

C005.narrative: Amy's school not yet affected — Block A excavation is 500m south of Tenderloin Community School.
Amy notices dust levels rising slightly when the wind blows from the south. Keeps windows slightly cracked for ventilation.
Rain suppresses construction dust for most of the month. Classroom air quality stable.
Block B start means construction is now directly adjacent to the school's outer wall. Amy begins monitoring on her air quality app.
Three asthma incidents in 6 weeks. PM2.5 spikes to 45 when trucks idle outside. Amy files a formal complaint with the DPW construction hotline.
DPW installs dust screens on the school-facing block perimeter. Some improvement. Amy keeps windows shut. The kids are restless in the heat.
Midpoint review commits to stopping jackhammer work during school hours from Month 8. Amy marks it in her classroom calendar.
Block B moves south of the school. Amy opens classroom windows for the first time in months. Kids cheer.
Amy's school files a formal request to be included in the post-construction community benefit plan — specifically requesting the school be added to the new street tree canopy commitment.
Amy hears about the $4M pipe burst overrun. Writes to her supervisor asking that $50,000 be allocated to classroom air filtration as a community benefit. No response yet.
Dust nearly gone. All classroom windows open. Three asthmatic students show improved peak flow readings at their checkups.
Amy's 3rd grade class plants two saplings in front of the school as part of the post-construction street tree replacement program. The kids name them.

C006.narrative: Tyler's protected cycle lane on Van Ness removed Day One. Now riding in mixed traffic with construction trucks. Starts wearing his helmet for the first time in two years.
Near-miss with a DoorDash delivery van near Fell Street. Tyler takes a photo of the scene and posts it. Gets 800 likes on Twitter.
Tyler works from home during the wet week of the atmospheric river. Starts researching Oakland co-working spaces during lunch.
Tyler attends a SFMTA community meeting and presents a 3-page cycling safety proposal for the interim construction period. Gets 5 minutes on the agenda.
SFMTA responds to Tyler's petition: cycling lanes will be 'considered' in the final design. Tyler screenshots it and captions it 'I'll believe it when I'm riding it.'
Tyler hires Luma Labs's first full-time employee. Takes her to dinner to celebrate. He cycles there — still dangerous, but he won't let them take that from him.
Tyler submits a detailed bike-lane design proposal to the DPW midpoint review panel. Gets 5 minutes on the agenda. Gets applause from two people.
Tyler learns Block C plans include a painted bike lane — not protected. He shares the plans on Mastodon and Reddit. Mixed response.
Tyler has another close call with a construction truck near Oak Street. That night he calls his contact at a Dogpatch co-working space in Oakland. Puts down a deposit.
Tyler is packing his desk at Hayes Valley. Tells his team they move to Oakland's Impact Hub from Month 11.
Tyler has moved Luma Labs to Oakland. Follows the Van Ness completion news from across the bay. Tweets: 'Hope the bike lane happens. Won't be riding it.'

Tyler watches the Van Ness ribbon-cutting livestream from his Oakland office. The painted bike lane is there. He tweets: 'Glad it's done. Painted lines aren't infrastructure. I'm still in Oakland.' He does not return.
